Pyrrhopappus carolinianus, also known as the Carolina Pyrrhopappus or the Carolina Flax, is a plant species in the family Asteraceae.

The health benefits of Pyrrhopappus carolinianus are not well-documented, but some Asteraceae species are known for their anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties. 

The bioactive constituents of Pyrrhopappus carolinianus are not well-studied, but some Asteraceae species contain flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids.

The most common herbal preparations made with Pyrrhopappus carolinianus are not well-known, but some Asteraceae species are used to make teas, tinctures, and infusions, often for their purported health benefits.
taxonomy: Pyrrhopappus grandiflorus is classified within the family Asteraceae. This family is commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. Pyrrhopappus grandiflorus is a species within the genus Pyrrhopappus. The genus Pyrrhopappus is part of the tribe Anthemideae. The tribe Anthemideae is a subtribe within the subfamily Asteroideae.
uses: Pyrrhopappus grandiflorus has been used in traditional medicine, particularly in Native American cultures, where its roots and leaves were used to treat various ailments. The plant has been used to treat fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. Its roots were also used to make a tea to help alleviate respiratory issues such as coughs and colds. In addition, the plant's leaves have been used to make a poultice to help soothe sore muscles and joints. Further research is needed to confirm the effectiveness and safety of using Pyrrhopappus grandiflorus for medicinal purposes.
